region_of_origin: Tropical regions; widely consumed across Southeast Asia including Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 226734001 refers to cassava as a substance; no specific boiled-cassava preparation code was identified in SNOMED CT. Closest parent match used as the substance-level code is the nearest available ancestor for the boiled preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled cassava is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one medium-sized piece, commonly consumed as a snack or side dish in Singapore and Southeast Asia.

nutrient_source.title: Cassava, boiled — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo database entry for boiled cassava (F14070018). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Cassava, boiled (GI ~94)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 94 for boiled cassava is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International GI table (Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002). Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (94 × 32.7 × 1.5) / 100 = 46.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (261 mg/100 g) support favourable blood pressure profile
hypertension.guidance: Boiled cassava is suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed in moderate portions (100–150 g) due to its negligible sodium content and potassium contribution of 261 mg/100 g, which supports vascular tone; however, its high carbohydrate density (32.7 g/100 g) warrants portion control to avoid excess caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~94) and high carbohydrate content (32.7 g/100 g) cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled cassava has a GI of approximately 94 and delivers 32.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g, resulting in a glycemic load of ~46 per 150 g serving; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portions to ≤50 g, pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt glycemic response, and consider lower-GI alternatives such as boiled sweet potato or legumes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and 5.0 g/100 g dietary fibre support a heart-healthy profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled cassava contains negligible saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and 5.0 g/100 g dietary fibre, which collectively support cardiovascular health; however, its high glycemic index may contribute to postprandial triglyceride elevation if consumed in large quantities, so portions should be kept to 100–150 g and combined with fibre-rich vegetables and lean protein.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre (5.0 g/100 g) supports gut microbiome and immune regulation; negligible saturated fat imposes no pro-inflammatory burden
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled cassava's 5.0 g/100 g dietary fibre supports gut microbiome health and immune regulation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; its negligible saturated fat and zero trans fat impose no pro-inflammatory burden, though high glycemic load at large portions may indirectly promote systemic inflammation and should be moderated.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled cassava (Manihot esculenta) is a starchy tuberous root vegetable that is peeled and simmered in water until soft and tender. It is a staple food across Southeast Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa, and Latin America, and is widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia as a snack or side dish. The flesh is white, dense, and mildly sweet with a soft, slightly fibrous texture after boiling. Per 100 g, boiled cassava provides 151 kcal, 32.7 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g protein, 0.3 g fat, 5.0 g dietary fibre, 2 mg sodium, and 261 mg potassium. Its glycemic index is approximately 94 (high), making it a high-glycemic food that can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with type 2 diabetes should consume it in very small portions (≤50 g) and pair it with protein and non-starchy vegetables. Despite its high GI, boiled cassava is low in sodium and cholesterol-free, making it suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular health in moderate portions. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher. In Singapore, it is commonly eaten as a snack, sometimes dusted with grated coconut or served plain. Also known as tapioca root, yuca, or manioc in various culinary traditions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in boiled cassava?
Is boiled cassava high in carbs?
What is the glycemic index of cassava?
Is cassava good for high blood pressure?
Is boiled tapioca the same as boiled cassava?
How much fibre does cassava have?
Can I eat cassava if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14070018) with confidence 0.95. GI value of 94 for boiled cassava is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International GI table (Foster-Powell et al., 2002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are complete and consistent with the approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for boiled cassava is not reported in the HPB Foodo database (F14070018) as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond potassium, calcium, and iron not available in the HPB Foodo record for this food item. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed zero as boiled cassava is a plain plant food with no added fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source; cassava is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol. saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.1 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cassava cooked (NDB 11134), consistent with the low total fat of 0.3 g/100 g reported by HPB. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g × serving_g) / 10000 = (94 × 32.7 × 150) / 10000 = 46.1. Ontology: FoodOn URI (FOODON_03301710) and FoodEx2 code (A00LC) are defensible exact matches for cassava boiled. SNOMED CT code 226734001 is used as a closest_parent match (substance-level ancestor) as no specific boiled-cassava preparation code exists in SNOMED CT.
